title: Reward-Guided Speculative Decoding for Efficient LLM Reasoning
affiliation: University of Amsterdam; Salesforce AI Research

background.problem_context: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve strong reasoning performance but at prohibitive inference cost. Speculative Decoding (SD) accelerates inference by using a lightweight draft model to generate candidate tokens verified by a target model, but enforces strict unbiasedness — the final token distribution must match the target model's. This constraint wastes high-quality draft tokens when their probabilities don't exactly match the target.
prior_limitations: Standard SD enforces unbiased sampling, rejecting useful draft tokens if their probability doesn't match the target's distribution
For reasoning tasks, strict token-level equivalence is unnecessary since the key information lies in semantic correctness of reasoning steps, not exact token sequences
SD cannot improve upon the target model's accuracy; it can only match it while reducing latency
Best-of-N sampling (BoN) requires N independent generations, scaling linearly in compute
background.key_observation: In reasoning tasks, a controlled bias toward high-reward outputs can be beneficial. Rather than enforcing distributional equivalence, we can prioritize outputs that achieve high process reward scores, even if they diverge from the target model's exact distribution.

motivation: Traditional speculative decoding rigidly enforces that the final token distribution matches the target model exactly (unbiasedness), which wastes high-quality draft tokens when their probabilities don't align with the target. For reasoning tasks, what matters is the semantic correctness of reasoning steps, not exact token distributions. By introducing controlled bias via a Process Reward Model (PRM), we can retain valuable draft model outputs and avoid unnecessary target model invocations, achieving better accuracy-cost tradeoffs than either SD or the target model alone.
novelty_claims: First framework to introduce controlled bias into speculative decoding via reward guidance, breaking the strict unbiasedness constraint for reasoning tasks
Theoretical proof that the optimal weighting under a sampling budget constraint is a binary step function (threshold-based), establishing an elegant threshold strategy
Demonstrates that RSD can simultaneously reduce computation (up to 4.4× fewer FLOPs) AND improve accuracy (+3.5% on average), unlike traditional SD which can only match the target
problem_statement: Given a lightweight draft model M_m and a powerful target model M_M, along with a Process Reward Model PRM, produce reasoning outputs that maximize quality (measured by PRM reward) while minimizing computational cost, without being constrained by the target model's exact token distribution.
core_idea: Define a mixture distribution P_RSD = w(y|z)·P_m(y|z) + ν·P_M(y|z) where w is a reward-based weighting function. Use PRM to score each intermediate reasoning step generated by the draft model; accept the step if its reward exceeds threshold δ, otherwise invoke the target model for regeneration. Theoretically prove that this threshold strategy is optimal under a fixed sampling budget.
extended_summary: RSD formalizes stepwise model routing as a controlled-bias speculative decoding problem. At each reasoning step, the draft model (e.g., 7B) generates a candidate step, which is scored by a PRM. If the PRM reward ≥ δ (a threshold), the step is accepted directly; otherwise, the target model (e.g., 72B) regenerates that step. The paper proves that under a constraint on the expected number of draft samples accepted (i.e., a sampling budget), the optimal weight function w*(y|z) is a binary step function — accept all drafts above a reward threshold, reject all below. This yields a simple yet theoretically optimal routing strategy. Experimentally, RSD achieves 88.0% on MATH-500 using 7B/72B/7B configuration (draft/target/PRM), surpassing the 72B standalone's 85.6% by +2.4% while using 4.4× fewer FLOPs. The threshold δ controls the accuracy-FLOPs tradeoff: lower δ accepts more draft steps (cheaper, slightly lower accuracy); higher δ routes more to the target (more expensive, higher accuracy). At δ=0.7, RSD outperforms the standalone target model in 7 of 8 configurations.

method_pipeline.name: Draft step generation
method_pipeline.description: The lightweight draft model M_m generates a complete reasoning step y given the current reasoning context z

method_pipeline.name: PRM reward scoring
method_pipeline.description: The Process Reward Model evaluates the quality of the draft step, producing a scalar reward r = PRM(y|z). Computational overhead is minimal (~equivalent to generating a few tokens)

method_pipeline.name: Threshold-based acceptance
method_pipeline.description: If r ≥ δ, accept the draft step. If r < δ, reject and invoke target model M_M to regenerate the step. δ controls the accuracy-cost tradeoff

method_pipeline.name: Target model regeneration (conditional)
method_pipeline.description: When the draft is rejected, the target model M_M generates its own step from the same context z. This step is always accepted

method_pipeline.name: Context update and iteration
method_pipeline.description: The accepted step (from draft or target) is appended to the context z, and the process repeats for the next reasoning step until a final answer is produced

design_choices.choice: Binary threshold acceptance rather than soft weighting
design_choices.rationale: Theoretically proven optimal under sampling budget constraint. The optimal weighting w*(y|z) under KL divergence constraint is a binary step function, making threshold-based acceptance not just a heuristic but the provably best strategy

Reasoning tasks decompose into semantically meaningful steps. Reward signals are meaningful at the step level, not token level. This also allows combination with token-level SD for hierarchical acceleration

design_choices.choice: PRM as reward signal rather than LLM-as-judge
design_choices.rationale: PRM is lightweight (can be as small as 1.5B), fast (single forward pass), and can potentially share parameters with the policy model. LLM-as-judge would be prohibitively expensive in the inner loop

evaluation.flops_definition: 2 × N_params × n_tokens per step, summed across all steps
evaluation.cost_model: Proportional to total FLOPs consumed by draft + target + PRM models

quantitative_results.notes: Surpasses target model by +2.4% with 4.4× fewer FLOPs
Average improvement over standalone target across all benchmarks
7 out of 8 configurations outperform standalone target model
48% of problems solved entirely by draft model without any target invocation

baselines_compared.delta: RSD achieves +2.4% accuracy at 4.4× fewer FLOPs on MATH-500
SD matches target accuracy but cannot exceed it; RSD exceeds target accuracy
RSD achieves comparable quality with significantly lower compute than BoN
+3.5% average accuracy improvement over parallel approaches

ablation_findings.effect: δ=0 equals draft-only; δ=1 equals target-only. δ=0.7 is the sweet spot for most configurations. Lower δ gives cheaper inference but slightly lower accuracy
ablation_findings.key_insight: The accuracy-cost curve is concave, meaning there's a region where small FLOPs increases yield large accuracy gains

ablation_findings.effect: RSD is robust to PRM selection. Different PRMs (Skywork vs Qwen2.5-Math) all consistently outperform SD
ablation_findings.key_insight: Even a 1.5B PRM provides meaningful reward signals for routing

ablation_findings.effect: Larger draft models (7B vs 1.5B) have higher acceptance rates, reducing target model calls but at higher per-step cost
ablation_findings.key_insight: Optimal draft size depends on target model size and task difficulty

ablation_findings.effect: Combining step-level RSD with token-level SD yields additional speedup
ablation_findings.key_insight: The two techniques are orthogonal and complementary

predecessor_comparison.detail: RSD generalizes SD by replacing the strict unbiasedness constraint with reward-guided controlled bias, enabling accuracy improvement rather than just cost reduction
RSD achieves similar quality benefits at much lower computational cost by using reward-guided accept/reject rather than generating N independent samples

qualitative_observations: For easy problems, the draft model's PRM scores are consistently high (≥0.7), allowing RSD to solve them entirely with the draft model — automatic difficulty-adaptive compute allocation
For hard problems (e.g., competition math), more steps are routed to the target model, but even these benefit from the draft model handling easier intermediate steps
Unlike SD, RSD never degrades below the target model's performance because it doesn't enforce distributional matching

efficiency.prm_overhead: Minimal — equivalent to generating a few additional tokens per step
efficiency.routing_latency: One PRM forward pass per step (~1-2ms)

main_findings: The optimal reward-based weighting for speculative decoding is a binary step function (threshold), proven theoretically under sampling budget constraint
RSD simultaneously reduces FLOPs (up to 4.4×) AND improves accuracy (+3.5% average) over the standalone target model — previously thought impossible with speculative methods
48% of problems are solved entirely by the draft model at δ=0.7, demonstrating automatic difficulty-adaptive compute allocation
RSD is robust to PRM choice and complementary to token-level speculative decoding
limitations: Requires access to a trained PRM, which limits applicability to domains where PRMs exist (primarily math reasoning)
Step-level routing assumes clean step boundaries (typically \n\n), which may not always be well-defined
The generate-then-verify paradigm incurs sunk costs when draft steps are rejected (both draft and PRM compute are wasted)
PRM quality directly bounds routing quality — noisy PRM scores lead to suboptimal routing decisions
Single-pass PRM evaluation may miss contextual dependencies between steps
future_work_by_authors: Combining RSD with other inference-time scaling techniques
Exploring non-threshold weighting strategies for specific task types

key_connections: RSD uses PRM as routing signal — our work proposes rubric-based process reward as a richer alternative to raw PRM scores
RSD's threshold-based routing is myopic (per-step) — our TRIM-Rubric uses RL to learn a policy considering multi-step trajectory quality
RSD's PRM evaluation is post-hoc (generate-then-verify) — our rubrics evaluate routing quality of the entire trajectory, not individual step quality
Our rubric reward complements rather than replaces PRM — PRM evaluates reasoning quality, rubric evaluates routing decision quality
relevance_to_our_work.advantage_over: RSD treats routing as a local decision (threshold on current step's PRM). Our work recognizes that good routing requires global trajectory reasoning (e.g., cascade error prevention, budget allocation across steps)
